4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Real-world demonstration of NUMA ContentionAs backend engineers, we are often taught that the ultimate solution for handling increased load is to “throw more hardware at the problem.” But what happens when upgrading your cloud infrastructure actually makes your application slower?
I recently tackled a high-throughput C# data-processing API deployed on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) that had hit a hard performance ceiling. It was averaging a 10-second response time under peak load. When I tried to vertically scale the AKS pods by giving them more compute, performance actually degraded.
Instead of moving faster, the system suffered from deep resource contention. Under high-core environments, the application spent more time managing CPU thread context switches and fighting over shared synchronous locks than executing actual business logic.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Mission Critical System Halting with increased resourcesWith the help of .NET concurrency optimizations, I was able to fix this issue -ultimately dropping response times from 10 seconds down to a highly responsive 3 seconds (a 70% optimization).
Here is a look behind the scenes at how I diagnosed the problem, re-architected the pipeline, and discovered a fascinating new scaling boundary along the way.
AI-Driven Profiling & Benchmarking
Rather than guessing where the bottlenecks lay, I built a highly intentional, data-driven diagnostic workflow:

• BenchmarkDotNet: I isolated the core execution paths using the standard .NET Benchmark library to establish a precise, repeatable performance baseline.

• GitHub Copilot @profiler: I used the @profiler agent to investigate the application's runtime behavior. The agent generated a comprehensive profiling report that immediately highlighted the lowest-risk, highest-gain improvements — specifically pinpointing severe thread pool starvation and intense lock contention on synchronous critical paths.
Agentic Coding with Cursor and Grok 4.5
To safely refactor the core execution pipeline without introducing structural regressions, I collaborated with Cursor’s Plan Mode powered by Grok 4.5. Read more about how Grok 4.5 makes my development easier here.
It allowed me to aggressively cross-question the AI’s proposed designs. Together, we simulated concurrent edge cases, evaluated lock-free alternatives, and verified that the new execution model would scale predictably under heavy multi-core environments.
Parallelism in C#
With the benchmrks in place, I completely re-engineered the data-processing algorithm:

• Transition to TPL Dataflow: I migrated shared mutable states to the Task Parallel Library (TPL), utilizing components like ActionBlock and TransformBlock to pass immutable messages through separate execution stages without blocking locks.

• Dynamic Concurrency Control: I enforced fine-grained parallelism dynamically tied to the container environment, ensuring the application could safely expand its thread usage based on the allocated AKS compute envelope:
var processingBlock = new ActionBlock<DataPayload>(
async payload => await ProcessDataAsync(payload),
new ExecutionDataflowBlockOptions
{
// Dynamically scales based on the allocated AKS core count
MaxDegreeOfParallelism = Environment.ProcessorCount * 2
});When I deployed the optimized microservice back to Azure AKS and monitored it, I observed a highly interesting scaling curve:

• Baseline Resources (8,000 mcores): The new version initially took 12 seconds (a slight increase from 10s due to the foundational architectural overhead of asynchronous orchestration). Initially, it looked like a loss.

• Vertically Scaled Resources (16,000 mcores): The moment I doubled the compute envelope, the architecture shined. Response times plummeted smoothly to 3 seconds or less. The system was finally scaling vertically!

• Excessive Resources (24,000 to 32,000 mcores): Curiously, performance began to degrade again at these massive core counts.
Why did performance degrade with too many resources?
In high-compute container environments, this is a known distributed systems phenomenon typically caused by Amdahl’s Law and NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) architecture switching.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Demonstration of Amdahl’s LawWhen a container spans too many physical CPU sockets or virtual cores, the overhead of the operating system coordinating thread scheduling across distinct hardware cache lines (inter-core communication latency) outpaces the computational work being done. In short: there is an absolute sweet spot for vertical scaling, and I found mine perfectly at 16,000 mcores.
Key Takeaways for Tech Leaders & Architects:

• Infrastructure Can’t Fix Friction: If your code suffers from architectural thread contention, upgrading your hardware will only make it fight itself faster.

• Cross-Question Your Tools: Using architectural planning layers like Cursor’s Plan Mode to vet designs before generation saves days of iterative debugging down the road.

• Find Your Compute Sweet Spot: More cores do not equal linear speed forever. Test your scaling boundaries to find the exact peak efficiency zone for your cloud budget.
How are you leveraging modern AI agent workflows to resolve deep system bottlenecks in your production stack? Let’s discuss in the comments! 👇
